Even if one disregard the intrinsic virtue of knowledge in Physics, it still motivate a lot of mathematics well. Nevertheless,
proper physics education is inadequate in undergraduate math programs at most institute. This note is my attempt to learn some
physics along with relevant basic mathematical tools. It is roughly equivalent to a note book in mathematical physics alongside
with introductory knowledge in classical mechanics, electrodynamics, quantum mechanics and statistical mechanics.

This note book includes code cells that is runnable in real-time. This provides more interactive visualization, as well as better reading
exprience since readers doesn’t need to copy code snippet and run in their own kernel. To run the code, always click the power buttom when
onto a page in this notebook. The underlying binder service would spwan a docker container which takes a minute or so. After the container is
up and running, one would see every code cell turning into an executable cell.

    #RUN THIS LINE. FIRST INITATE THE KERNEL BY CLICK THE POWER BUTTON ON TOP ⬆️
    print("This is an online mechanics note with executable python code!")

An electric field \mathbf{E} is a vector field \mathbb{R}^{n}\to \mathbb{R}^{n}, where \mathbf{E}(\vec x_0) is the force experienced by a unit charge(positive test charge) at location \vec x_0.\mathbf{E}(\vec x_0)=\frac{1}{4\pi \epsilon_0} \int \frac{\rho(\vec x)}{\|\vec x_0-\vec x\|^3}(\vec x_0-\vec x) d\vec x

where \rho gives the charge density at the location.

Which simply comes from the coulomb’s law. The integral is written with a general sense which can be a summation in the discrete case and a  Riemann integral in other cases.The next two examples illustrate this idea.

Consider a cube placed at the origin with edge length 1 a point charge of -1C at each vertex. What is the resulting electric field?\mathbf{E}(\vec x_0)=-\frac{1}{4\pi \epsilon_0 }\sum_{i,j,k\in \{-1/2,1/2\}}\frac{(x_0-i,y_0-j,z_0-k)^T}{\|\vec (x_0-i,y_0-j,z_0-k)\|^3}

Which can be easily simulated:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy as sp

def compute_field_for_point_charge(x0, X, Y, Z):
    """compute the field induced by the unit point charge x in the scope

    Args:
        x (NdArray): (x0,y0,z0) coordinate of the charge 
        X/Y/Z (NdArray): meshgrid for scope
    """
    C = 4 * np.pi * sp.constants.epsilon_0
    #separation vector
    R_x = X - x0[0] 
    R_y = Y - x0[1]
    R_z = Z - x0[2]
    R = np.stack([R_x, R_y, R_z])
    mag = np.linalg.norm(R, ord=2, axis=0)
    mask = np.random.binomial(n=1, p=mag/(np.max(mag)+1e-8),size=X.shape)
    R = R * mask
    return R  / (C * mag)**3



N_sample=6
scope = np.linspace(-0.75, 0.75, N_sample)
X, Y, Z = np.meshgrid(scope, scope, scope)
final_field = np.empty(shape=(3, N_sample, N_sample, N_sample))
fig, ax = plt.subplots(subplot_kw={'projection':'3d'}) 
for i in (-1/2,  1/2):
    for j in (-1/2,  1/2):
        for k in (-1/2,  1/2):
            final_field -=  compute_field_for_point_charge(np.array([i,j,k]), X, Y, Z)
            ax.scatter(i,j,k, c='r', marker='o')
ax.quiver(X, Y, Z,  final_field[0], final_field[1], final_field[2], normalize=True, length=1e-1)
plt.show()
